title: Jesus Christ — The Final Revelation: Introduction to Hebrews
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.title: Introduction: Purpose of This Series
subpoints: In two weeks, a 15-part immersive series through Hosea begins (morning and afternoon each Sunday); before that, a three-part series on Hebrews 1:1-4 to provide Christological lenses through which to read Hosea
Understanding Jesus Christ as the ultimate and final revelation of God will give us the lens to read the Old Testament as Christians, not as Jews
Hebrews has special significance to the pastor: through the preaching of Hebrews and personal reading of Romans, God chose to save him when he first came to the United States
Method: four questions about any book of the Bible — who wrote it? to whom? why? when? — demonstrated with Hebrews; model for how to study any book of the Bible

outline.title: 1. Who Wrote Hebrews?
subpoints: Hebrews has no epistolary introduction — unlike all other New Testament letters except 1 John; the book reads like a sermon, not a letter (Hebrews 13:22 calls it a "word of exhortation" — the same term used for apostolic preaching in Acts 13)

The human author cannot be determined with certainty; many names have been proposed throughout history; Origen (3rd century): "the thoughts are the apostle's [Paul's] but the style and composition are the work of someone else... as to who actually wrote the epistle, God knows the truth of the matter"

What we can determine from the text: (1) it was a man — Hebrews 11:32 uses a grammatically masculine form; (2) he was a second-generation Christian — Hebrews 2:1-3 places a generation between himself and the apostles; (3) he had a great pastoral heart — the word "therefore" appears 28 times, making constant application; (4) he had a high view of Scripture and understood it as God's word incarnated in the Son (Hebrews 1:1-3, 4:12); (5) he was a Greek-speaking Jewish Christian — Jewish because of how deeply he valued the Old Testament; Christian because he reads it through Christ; Greek-speaking because he quotes from the Septuagint rather than the Hebrew text; (6) in summary: a Bible-saturated, brethren-loving, word-applying, Greek-speaking pastor

outline.title: 2. To Whom Was Hebrews Written?
subpoints: The title "To the Hebrews" was added early (not original to the letter) but reflects an accurate tradition

Evidence from the text: the overwhelming number of Old Testament quotes indicates an audience that held the Old Testament in high esteem (contrast Paul's synagogue sermons in Acts 13 — full of OT quotes — with his sermon at the Areopagus in Acts 17 — no OT quotes); the people and institutions discussed (Moses, the Aaronic priesthood, the tabernacle, the heroes of faith in chapter 11) are those important to Jewish culture

These were likely Jewish Christians living in the diaspora (outside Palestine) — probably in Rome: chapter 13:24 — "those from Italy greet you" — indicates that people originally from Italy were writing from outside it to people in Rome

outline.title: 3. Why Was Hebrews Written?
subpoints: The pastor was seriously concerned that these people — whom he loved — were about to abandon Christ and return to pre-Christ Judaism

They had suffered acute persecution early in their faith (Hebrews 10:32-34) — endured a great struggle with joy, accepted the plundering of their goods, knowing they had a better possession in heaven; but no lives were lost

Now the initial excitement had worn off; persecution had subsided; they were in the day-to-day grind of Christian life; and being a Christian meant social and financial ruin in their close Jewish community — shunned by family, dissociated from the temple and Jewish life

Interesting that it is the normalcy of life — not great adversity — that causes these doubts; like the husband who says he has no problem dying for his wife but struggles with the daily self-sacrifice of living for her
Is Christ worth it? The pastor answers by repeatedly commanding: consider him — Hebrews 3:1-3, 7:4, 12:3
There is no such thing as worship of the God of the Bible outside of Christ; going back to pre-Christ Judaism means worshiping a false god; the Father and the Son cannot be separated

outline.title: 4. When Was Hebrews Written?
subpoints: The author is second-generation, so after 50 AD; the evidence for location in Rome (chapter 13:24) and the time of the only sustained persecution of Roman Christians (Nero, mid-60s AD) both point to the mid-60s
The temple was destroyed in AD 70; the author argues against returning to the sacrificial system of the Old Testament, which only makes sense if the temple still exists; confirms a date before AD 70
Putting it together: written around AD 65-67 to Jewish Christians in Rome contemplating abandoning Christianity to return to a form of Judaism that would effectively disappear in a few years

outline.title: 5. The Theme and Five Warnings of Hebrews
subpoints: The single theme of the epistle: the necessity of persevering faith in Christ; if you get nothing else, get this — persevere in faith in Christ

First warning (Hebrews 2:1-4): the danger of neglect — how shall we escape if we neglect so great a salvation? When people struggle with faith, they close the Bible; the Spirit says: go deeper into this book

Second warning (Hebrews 3:7-13): the danger of unbelief — do not harden your hearts as in the rebellion; exhort one another daily; when struggling with faith, people also distance themselves from other Christians — the Spirit says: you need people in your life to point out your blind spots, to exhort you daily

Third warning (Hebrews 4:11-13): the danger of disobedience — the word of God is living and powerful, sharper than a two-edged sword, discerning thoughts and intents; God knows what is in your heart; stop thinking of yourself as condemned to sin — as those who have the Spirit, you are empowered to obey

Fourth warning (Hebrews 5:11-6:6): the danger of immaturity — these people had become dull of hearing; willful immaturity is not stagnancy but regression; you cannot be satisfied with never growing in Christ

Fifth warning (Hebrews 10:26-31): the danger of rejection — if we sin willfully after receiving the knowledge of the truth (i.e., if we reject Christ), there no longer remains a sacrifice for sins; how much worse punishment for the one who has trampled the Son of God underfoot; it is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God
How do we persevere? By looking to Jesus, the author and perfecter of our faith; through the means: Bible reading, preaching, sacraments, fellowship, prayer — all as ways of looking to Jesus in faith

summary: This sermon introduces a new series on Hebrews 1:1-4, described as a three-part introduction to be followed by an immersive 15-part series through Hosea. The series aims to equip the congregation to read the Old Testament through the Christological lens of Hebrews — to see how Jesus Christ is the ultimate and final revelation of God. Pastor Lyro works through four introductory questions about the book. Who wrote it? Hebrews has no author named and reads as a sermon (called a "word of exhortation" in 13:22); the human author cannot be determined with certainty (Origen: God knows), but can be described as a male second-generation Christian with a pastoral heart, a high view of Scripture, and a Greek-speaking Jewish-Christian background — a Bible-saturated, brethren-loving, word-applying pastor. To whom? Likely Jewish Christians in Rome (the diaspora), indicated by the heavy Old Testament quotation, the Jewish institutions discussed, and the phrase "those from Italy greet you" in chapter 13. Why? The pastor feared these believers were about to abandon Christ and return to pre-Christ Judaism under the pressure not of persecution (they had endured that) but of the daily grind of Christian life — social and financial exclusion from their Jewish community. The sermon repeatedly commands: consider him (Hebrews 3:1, 7:4, 12:3). When? Mid-60s AD, before the temple's destruction in AD 70, during the Neronian persecution in Rome. The sermon then identifies the single theme of Hebrews — persevering faith in Christ — and surveys all five warning passages, closing with the call to persevere by looking to Jesus through the means of grace.
transcript:
## Introduction: Reading Hebrews Before Reading Hosea

In two weeks, Lord willing, we will start a 15-part series on the prophecy of Hosea. It is going to be an immersive series — we will go through the whole book, morning and afternoon every Sunday, until we have covered all 15 lessons. Before we get to Hosea, I want to take you on a three-part series through just these four verses — Hebrews 1:1-4.

The reading for that — the reason for this introduction — is that understanding Jesus Christ as the ultimate and final revelation of God in the pages of the New Testament will give us the lenses through which we will be able to look at the book of Hosea. We do not want to read anything apart from the life, death, resurrection, ascension, and session of the Lord Jesus Christ. We do not want to read the Old Testament like a Jew would read it. We want to read it as Christians, with the understanding that Christ is the fulfillment and the ultimate revelation of God.

Hebrews has a special place in my heart. It was through the preaching of Hebrews and my personal reading of Romans that God chose to save me. When I first came to the United States, Pastor Leaman was preaching through the book of Hebrews. A couple months into that, alongside my own Bible reading, the Lord chose to save me.

This morning I want to introduce the whole book by asking four questions — questions that can be asked of any book of the Bible. Who wrote it? To whom was it written? Why was it written? When was it written?

## 1. Who Wrote Hebrews?

All the New Testament letters begin with an introduction — who is writing, to whom, with a greeting — except two: 1 John and Hebrews. Hebrews goes straight into its content. And except for chapter 13 verses 18-25, Hebrews does not follow the format of a letter at all. It is structured as a sermon.

In Hebrews 13:22, the author himself tells us this: he calls the book a "word of exhortation." This exact expression is used in Acts 13 when Paul and Barnabas are in Syrian Antioch and the leaders of the synagogue say, "Come and give us a word of exhortation" — meaning, preach to us. So what we call the Epistle to the Hebrews is in fact a sermon written to a particular congregation.

Because it has no author named, we cannot know with certainty who the human author was. This did not stop scholars from guessing — almost every person named in the New Testament has been proposed as a candidate. Origen of Alexandria, writing in the third century, gave the most honest assessment: "For my own part, if I may state my opinion, I should say that the thoughts are the apostle's [Paul's], but the style and composition are the work of someone who called to mind the apostle's teaching and wrote short notes on what his master said. As for who actually wrote the epistle, God knows the truth of the matter."

What we can learn about the author from the text itself:

First: it was a man. Hebrews 11:32 uses a grammatically masculine form — the word "tell" is written in a way that the speaker was male. That is clear in the original Greek.

Second: he was a second-generation Christian. Hebrews 2:1-3 — he puts a generation between himself and the apostles: "which at first began to be spoken by the Lord, and was confirmed to us by those who heard him." The Lord spoke first; the apostles then confirmed it; it was then passed to us. He never personally met the Lord during his earthly ministry.

Third: he had a great pastoral heart. The word "therefore" appears 28 times in this epistle — used in every chapter except chapter 5. Why is that significant? "Therefore" is the application word. He teaches a truth about God, and then says: this is how you live; this is how you believe. He does not wait for the end of the book to apply — he applies throughout.

Fourth: he had a very high regard for the word of God. Hebrews 1:1-3 — these are not the words of man but the words of God, spoken by God and incarnated in the Son. And Hebrews 4:12 — *the word of God is living and powerful, sharper than any two-edged sword, piercing even to the division of soul and spirit, of joints and marrow, and is a discerner of the thoughts and intents of the heart.* The word is so powerful that in order for you to know yourself, you need to know yourself through this word.

Fifth: he quoted and explained 39 passages of the Old Testament from 10 different books. He understood that the word must be expounded, proclaimed, and explained to the people.

Sixth: he was probably a Greek-speaking Jewish Christian. Jewish because of how much he values the Old Testament. Christian because he sees it through the eyes of Christ. Greek-speaking because, unlike many other New Testament authors, he quotes consistently from the Greek translation of the Old Testament (the Septuagint) rather than the Hebrew text.

Putting it all together: a Bible-saturated, brethren-loving, word-applying, Greek-speaking pastor — writing on the inspiration of the Holy Spirit.

## 2. To Whom Was Hebrews Written?

The title "To the Hebrews" was added early — not original to the letter, but probably added when the letter was first copied. It reflects an early and accurate tradition.

From the text: the overwhelming amount of Old Testament quotation indicates an audience that held the Old Testament in high esteem. Compare Paul's synagogue sermon in Acts 13 — almost every other verse is a quote from the Old Testament — with his sermon at the Areopagus in Acts 17, delivered to pagan philosophers — no Old Testament quotes at all. The fact that this author piles on Old Testament quotation tells us that his audience thought highly of it.

The people and institutions central to the argument of the letter — Moses, the Aaronic priesthood, the tabernacle, the heroes of chapter 11 — are things of great importance to Jewish people. All of this points to Jewish Christians.

They were likely living in the diaspora — outside Palestine. And probably in Rome specifically. In chapter 13:24, the author says, "Those from Italy greet you." This indicates that people originally from Italy, who are no longer in Italy — who have come with the author — are sending greetings back to the church. That church is in Rome.

## 3. Why Was Hebrews Written?

The pastor was seriously concerned that these people — whom he loved deeply — were about to abandon Christ and return to pre-Christ Judaism.

They had suffered. Hebrews 10:32-34: "Recall the former days in which, after you were illuminated, you endured a great struggle with sufferings — partly while you were made a spectacle by reproaches and tribulations, and partly while you became companions of those who were so treated. For you had compassion on me in my chains, and joyfully accepted the plundering of your goods, knowing that you have a better and an enduring possession for yourselves in heaven."

They had faced acute persecution when they first came to the gospel and endured it with joy and thanksgiving. No lives were lost. But now, the initial excitement of meeting Christ has worn off; the persecution has somewhat subsided; and these Jewish Christians are in the day-to-day grind of being a Christian. And every day of ordinary life as a Christian meant social and financial exclusion from their close Jewish community — shunned by family and fellow Jews, no longer connected to the temple and the life they had always known.

Now that life was regular — not a crisis, just every day — they started struggling with their faith. Is Christ worth it? What would life be like without him? What was life better without Christ?

It is interesting that it is the normalcy of life that brings the doubts. It seems like we can face great adversity and our faith is strengthened through it. But in the ordinary normalcy of life we tend to doubt our Savior. I once heard a pastor say: I have no problem dying for my wife. It is living for her that is complicated. We husbands would jump in front of a bullet for our wives without a second thought. But the everyday, moment-by-moment selfless sacrifice — that is the struggle.

Is Christ worth it? And this faithful pastor answers throughout: consider him. Hebrews 3:1-3 — *consider the Apostle and High Priest of our confession, Christ Jesus.* Hebrews 7:4 — *consider how great this man was.* Hebrews 12:3 — *consider him who endured such hostility from sinners against himself, lest you become weary and discouraged in your souls.*

Is Christ worth it? Yes. He is the author and completer of our faith. He conquered death through death. He brings us to the Father on account of his own life and resurrection.

And there is no such thing as worshiping the God of the Bible outside of Christ. Going back to pre-Christ Judaism — worshiping the God of the Old Testament while rejecting Christ — is worshiping a false god. The Father and the Son are inseparable. The God of the Old Testament is the one who reveals himself in Jesus Christ and says that no one comes to him except through the Son. You can repent of that right now and worship him through Christ.

## 4. When Was Hebrews Written?

The author is second-generation, so after AD 50. The evidence for the audience being in Rome places them during the Neronian persecution — the mid-60s. Nero's persecution was localized to the city of Rome and is historically identified as the context of the suffering described in chapter 10.

And crucially: the author argues against returning to the sacrificial system of the Old Testament. That only makes sense if the temple still stands. The temple was destroyed in AD 70. So this letter was written before AD 70 — probably around AD 65-67.

The congregation was thinking about returning to a form of Judaism that was, within a few years, going to effectively disappear from history.

## 5. The Theme and Five Warnings

The single theme of Hebrews: the necessity of persevering faith in Christ. If you get nothing else from this book, get this: persevere in faith in Christ. These people were seriously considering abandoning Christ, and there is no other option. Only through Christ can one continue to live for the glory of God.

Throughout the letter the author gives five warnings about the result of abandoning Christ.

First warning, chapter 2:1-4 — the danger of neglect. *How shall we escape if we neglect so great a salvation?* When people are struggling with their faith, the first thing they do is close the Bible and put it away. And yet the Holy Spirit says: if you are struggling, go deeper into this book, not away from it.

Second warning, chapter 3:7-13 — the danger of unbelief. *Do not harden your hearts as in the rebellion.* Beware, brethren, lest any of you have an evil heart of unbelief in departing from the living God. Exhort one another daily, while it is called "today." When people are struggling with their faith, they also distance themselves from other Christians. Here the Holy Spirit says: you need people in your life. You need daily exhortation to see Jesus, to know what is going on in your heart, to confess your sins.

Third warning, chapter 4:11-13 — the danger of disobedience. *Be diligent to enter that rest, lest anyone fall according to the same example of disobedience. For the word of God is living and powerful, sharper than any two-edged sword.* God knows what is in your heart. Stop thinking of yourself as condemned to sin every time you face temptation. You have the Spirit of God; you are a saint; you are empowered to obey.

Fourth warning, chapter 5:11-6:6 — the danger of immaturity. *You have become dull of hearing.* Willful immaturity is not stagnancy but regression. It is the sign of not being a true believer in Jesus Christ. You cannot be satisfied with never growing in Christ — it is not acceptable for a forty-year-old to still be in diapers.

Fifth warning, chapter 10:26-31 — the danger of rejection. *For if we sin willfully after we have received the knowledge of the truth, there no longer remains a sacrifice for sins, but a certain fearful expectation of judgment.* How much worse punishment do you think he will deserve who has trampled the Son of God underfoot? It is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God.

If you reject Christ, God will reject you. That is a certainty.

How do we persevere? By looking to Jesus, the author and perfecter of our faith. There is no other way to persevere. Through the means — Bible reading, preaching, sacraments, fellowship, prayer — we persevere by looking to Jesus in faith, hoping for the day when we will see him as he is, for we shall be like him.
